data.title: Columbia Threadneedle Fixed-Income Monitor: January 2026
data.description:
By Samantha Parsons

Determining how to allocate to fixed income requires careful consideration. Our monthly Fixed-Income Monitor consolidates the views of our investment team to help identify opportunities and risks across the asset class.

Key takeaways for January 2026

  • The Federal Reserve (Fed) delivered a 25-basis-point (bp) cut in December, lowering the target range to 3.50%-3.75%. This marked the third reduction of 2025, totaling 75 bps for the year. Fed Chair Jerome Powell communicated caution on future cuts amid inflation concerns.
  • U.S. Treasury yields showed muted reactions, as markets had already priced in the decision. Longer term rates remained elevated given uncertainty about future cuts.
  • The (delayed) November unemployment rate rose to 4.6%, the highest in four years, alongside a modest gain of 64,000 payrolls, further suggesting a gradual cooling in the labor market.
  • After early-month volatility, corporate credit posted solid gains. Investment-grade bonds outperformed high yield, supported by lower Treasury rates and strong fundamentals. Spreads tightened across sectors, signaling investor confidence despite macro uncertainty, but security selection remains key.

Yields and credit spread tracker

The proprietary fixed-income tracker compares yields and credit spreads over 20 years of history and across fixed income, which can help investors evaluate how they are being compensated for taking on risk.1

Source: Columbia Threadneedle Investments as of December 15, 2025. Each bar represents the range for the last 20 years, with the current percentile position indicated. Yield is represented by yield to worst, which is the minimum return received on a callable bond, apart from the yield if the issuer were to default. For municipal bonds, yield is represented by taxable equivalent yield, which is based on the top federal bracket (37%) and net investment income tax (3.8%). Other taxes are possible. Yield percentile and spread percentile are represented by the range of daily yields and daily spreads, respectively, and both are for the last 20 years, with the current percentile position indicated. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results.

Fixed income sector positioning

Our fixed income team analyzes fundamentals, valuations and technicals to assess relative value in bond markets. This approach can help investors position for risks and opportunities across sectors within the asset class.

The bottom line

Fixed income plays a crucial role in a well-diversified portfolio. Our investment team uses bottom-up company and sector analysis with top-down macroeconomic analysis to build fixed-income portfolios.

Fixed income portfolio allocations should reflect personal financial goals and risk tolerance; a financial advisor can help tailor a strategy to fit an individual investor’s needs.

Disclosures

1.Interpreting yields and spreads: Spread refers to the difference between a bond’s yield and the yield of a risk-free issue with the same duration (e.g., U.S. Treasuries), and indicates how much investors are being compensated for taking on additional credit (default) risk. If spreads are above their long-term average (sometimes called "wide" or "cheap"), investors are paid more to take on credit risk; if they're below their long-term average (called "narrow" or "tight"), investors are being paid less. There's no credit spread on U.S. Treasuries, which are considered risk-free government securities, since payments are guaranteed by the U.S. government.

Agency MBS is represented by the Morgan Stanley 30-Year Conventional Current Coupon ZV Indicator, which represents the zero volatility spread for the hypothetical $100-priced 30-year conventional mortgage over time. Investment grade is represented by the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Investment Grade Index. High yield is represented by the Bloomberg U.S. High Yield Corporate Bond Index. Municipal bonds are represented by the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index. It is not possible to invest directly in an index. Index descriptions are available here.

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. There are risks associated with fixed-income investments, including credit risk, interest rate risk, and prepayment and extension risk. In general, bond prices rise when interest rates fall and vice versa. This effect is usually more pronounced for longer term securities. A rise in interest rates may result in a price decline of fixed-income instruments. Falling rates may result in a fund investing in lower yielding debt instruments, lowering income and yield. These risks may be heightened for longer maturity and duration securities. Mortgage- and asset-backed securities are affected by interest rates, financial health of issuers/originators, creditworthiness of entities providing credit enhancements and the value of underlying assets. Income from tax-exempt municipal bonds or municipal bond funds may be subject to state and local taxes, and a portion of income may be subject to the federal and/or state alternative minimum tax for certain investors. Federal and state income tax rules will apply to any capital gains.

© 2016-2026 Columbia Threadneedle. All rights reserved.

Use of products, materials and services available through Columbia Threadneedle Investments may be subject to approval by your home office.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund carefully before investing. To learn more about this and other important information about each fund, download a free prospectus. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing.

The views expressed are as of the date given, may change as market or other conditions change and may differ from views expressed by other Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC (CMIA) associates or affiliates. Actual investments or investment decisions made by CMIA and its affiliates, whether for its own account or on behalf of clients, may not necessarily reflect the views expressed. This information is not intended to provide investment advice and does not take into consideration individual investor circumstances. Investment decisions should always be made based on an investor's specific financial needs, objectives, goals, time horizon and risk tolerance. Asset classes described may not be appropriate for all investors. Past performance does not guarantee future results, and no forecast should be considered a guarantee either. Since economic and market conditions change frequently, there can be no assurance that the trends described here will continue or that any forecasts are accurate.

Columbia Funds are distributed by Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc., member FINRA and are managed by Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC. Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC serves as the investment manager to the ETFs. ALPS Distributors, Inc. (Member FINRA) is the distributor for Columbia Threadneedle Investments ETFs. Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc., (Member FINRA) is a marketing agent for the ETFs. ALPS Distributors, Inc. is not affiliated with Columbia Threadneedle Investments.

Columbia Threadneedle Investments (Columbia Threadneedle) is the global brand name of the Columbia and Threadneedle group of companies.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 01/23/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Energy Stocks Steady Amid Macro Chaos, With A Sunday Night Earnings Surprise Ahead
data.description:
It was a sea of red to kick off the holiday-shortened trading week yesterday. President Trump’s ambition to annex part or all of Greenland drew backlash from European leaders. A new set of tariffs also roiled markets, precisely one year after the 47th POTUS took office.

In terms of market action, the Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) spiked above 20, while the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) suffered its worst session since last August. But it wasn’t solely a U.S.-Europe macro story.

The Land of the Rising Sun.... And Falling Fixed Income Market

An outright bond market implosion occurred in Japan. The island nation’s 40-year yield skyrocketed to a record above 4.20%, and its benchmark 30-year rate jumped 27 basis points to a fresh all-time high of 3.88%. Worries over tax cuts and their impact on Japan’s fiscal situation continued a macro trend ongoing for the past year-plus.

All eyes will be on the upcoming Bank of Japan monetary policy meeting, ahead of the U.S. Federal Reserve’s January 28 interest-rate decision.

Energy Atop the Early-Year Sector Leaderboard, High Macro Volatility

For investors, 10 of the 11 S&P 500 sectors traded in the red yesterday morning. The lone bright spot? Energy.

The oil & gas space often offers diversification benefits to global portfolio managers when macro jitters take center stage. Shares of Exxon Mobil (XOM), the world’s most valuable publicly traded energy company, tagged a record high above $131 for the first time. It has been remarkable how resilient large energy firms have been, given depressed oil prices.

Indeed, both WTI and Brent crude have felt the brunt of a global oil glut. President Trump’s “drill, baby, drill” mindset and OPEC's unsuccessful attempts to stabilize the supply-demand balance have kept oil bears in charge.

Though prices spiked earlier this month when the U.S. captured Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro (it feels like ages ago at this point), prices quickly fell back down. American households probably aren’t complaining, as AAA reports that the average cost of a gallon of regular unleaded is near five-year lows at $2.82.

Don’t Forget about Natural Gas: Old Man Winter Tightens His Grip

Stable oil prices contrast what’s happening in the natural gas markets. The February 2026 contract of U.S. Henry Hub gas jumped nearly 30% on Tuesday. Forecasts for bone-chilling cold over the final week of January spooked markets, and this weekend’s wintry blast could rival what transpired in Texas four years ago, just as power grids grapple with rising prices and pressure from policymakers. Impacts are likely from the Lone Star State to the Mid-Atlantic.

Earnings Events Add Drama and Investor Interest, BKR Stands Out

So, there’s no shortage of drama at home and abroad, as well as across oil and natural gas markets, for energy executives to weigh. Near-term risks and long-term opportunities are aplenty at the moment.

That will make the upcoming round of energy sector Q4 reports all the more interesting. Additionally, our team uncovered an outlier company that will report off schedule this coming Sunday night.

What is that firm? Baker Hughes (BKR). Shares of the $41 billion market-cap oil and gas equipment and services company hover near record highs ahead of its unusual weekend earnings event. There hasn’t been significant news lately - we have to go back to last summer, when BKR acquired Chart Industries (GTLS) in a $13.6 billion M&A move.

Market pricing also suggests that the January 25 Q4 report might not include many fireworks. According to data from Option Research & Technology Services, the stock is expected to move just 4.8% (near its long-term average earnings-related straddle of 4.9%). Shares dipped 3.3% following the October Q3 report.1

We also dug into the corporate event calendar. BKR’s quarterly conference call takes place Monday morning next week, but a quiet stretch then ensues - no major volatility catalysts until its April Q1 report. Nevertheless, be on guard for potential one-time items to be included in Sunday’s release.

Listen Up!

Beyond Baker Hughes, and after Halliburton (HAL) reported on Wednesday morning, SLB Corp. (SLB), formerly known as Schlumberger, serves up numbers Friday before the bell.

The heavy hitters - XOM and Chevron (CVX) - report next Friday, January 30. Investors will listen intently to what CEOs Darren Woods and Mike Wirth have to say about the volatile state of the global energy market, particularly regarding Venezuela.

Opportunities in South America?

Much has been made of the country’s oil reserves, which are likely materially lower than what the Maduro and former Chávez regimes have claimed. Moreover, while resource-rich, Venezuelan oil is notoriously sour and dense, more like tar than domestic light sweet crude.

In short, establishing a further U.S. presence there will take years, so don’t expect any concrete developments to be unveiled next Friday morning. Still, Chevron’s existing Venezuela assets could be optimized to extract more “black gold” in the quarters ahead.

Bulls Gaining Steam

As for price action, XOM and CVX are up almost 9% so far in 2026, outperforming the S&P 500 by more than eight percentage points. Momentum is solid, and income investors may be drawn to those stocks’ dividend yields (3.2% for XOM and 4.1% for CVX).

Elsewhere, the energy conference tour heats up in March. Three notable events take place toward the end of Q1:

  • March 2: Jefferies Flagship Power, Utilities, Clean Energy & Energy Conference

  • March 16: Piper Sandler 26th Annual Energy Conference

  • March 23: CERA Week

The Bottom Line

The Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) leads among the 11 S&P 500 sector funds. Up more than 6% on the year, shares of oil & gas companies have offered decent diversification amid a very busy January on the macro front.

The coming days will bring company-specific story updates, including an unusual Sunday-night earnings event. Next week’s XOM and CVX Q4 reports will be particularly revealing, given early-year geopolitical developments.

1 BKR, ORATS, January 21, 2026, ORATS Dashboard

Original Post

data.title: IGV Vs. IGPT: Performance Gap Shows AI Is Starting To Eat Software
data.description:
For years, two software ETFs moved almost in lockstep. That changed recently. These ETFs are the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) and the Invesco AI and Next Gen Software ETF (IGPT). Over the past year, IGPT has outperformed IGV by about 33 percentage points on a total-return basis, with most of the divergence occurring over the past six months. This may reflect the market's fear that software SaaS companies' moats are weakening, while AI picks-and-shovel stocks get favored as key beneficiaries of the data center boom.

One caveat is that IGPT shows a long price history, but in reality the ETF had a strategy change in August 2023. This involved renaming the ticker from PSJ to the current IGPT. The change also meant replacing the underlying reference benchmark with the "STOXX World AC NexGen Software Development Index". The strategy change for the ETF started in August 2023, making it significantly more "AI focused". This resulted in exposure to more chip and hardware companies that have benefited from the AI theme. Even after the change in strategy for IGPT, both ETFs had similar performances. As seen in the graph below, most of the performance difference has occurred in the last six months.

Software's Moat Under Attack

Software companies have historically earned strong competitive moats for a few reasons. Switching costs are a key one. Changing a core platform can be both technically and operationally risky.

Some businesses also benefit from network effects. Microsoft (MSFT) is a good example, as many organizations use Office and Windows because most employees are already familiar with them, and employees learn them because so many employers require them. In other cases, proprietary datasets, integrations, or patents add further strength to the competitive moats.

We believe the "AI impact" on moats will be different for each company, and we discussed some examples in a previous article of companies at risk of disruption. AI coding is lowering the barriers to entry, reducing the time and costs required to build complex software. This could result in companies building their own tools, or startups building competing software at significantly reduced prices compared to the incumbents. AI agents can also help migrate data from one system to another, reducing switching costs. New AI-native startups could also ship software more quickly, and be more aggressive on pricing.

Portfolio Characteristics

There are some important differences in the portfolio characteristics of both ETFs that help explain the recent performance divergence. Both have significant exposure to software companies, but IGPT's largest sector weight is semiconductors.

This can be seen in the top holdings. IGPT includes several chip and semiconductor equipment names that have been strong performers, while IGV's top positions skew more toward application software. The ETFs also differ in their "Mag 7" exposure. Both have meaningful weight in mega-cap tech, but IGPT carries more of it, whereas IGV's current top-ten list is dominated by software names, with Microsoft standing out as the main mega-cap in the top holdings. Adobe (ADBE) is the only top-ten overlap between the two, and IGPT's top holdings include tech-heavy communications sector exposure such as Alphabet (GOOGL) and Meta Platforms (META).

Top 10 Holdings IGV Top 10 Holdings IGPT
Microsoft Micron Technology
Palantir Technologies Alphabet Inc
Oracle Advanced Micro Devices
Salesforce NVIDIA
AppLovin Meta Platforms
Intuit SK hynix
Palo Alto Networks Intuitive Surgical
Adobe Intel
CrowdStrike QUALCOMM
ServiceNow Adobe

Future Outlook

The market appears to have taken the disruption risk for software companies more seriously starting in the second half of last year. This year fears have intensified as AI coding tools have gotten better. NVIDIA's Huang even praised Anthropic's (ANTHRO) Claude at Davos, saying they use it "all over" at NVIDIA.

Earnings projections for software companies are projected to decelerate, while chip makers are expected to see accelerated growth. This has led to the performance divergence continuing between IGV and IGPT year-to-date. The iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) has performed even better, with an over 15% total return this year.

It is our opinion that the disruption threat for some software companies is real, but they should be evaluated on an individual basis. Certain companies are more entrenched and difficult to displace than others. The market appears to be punishing companies in a relatively indiscriminate manner, with the likes of Intuit (INTU), Adobe, and Salesforce (CRM), all plunging after new coding tools were released this year.

Valuations

Despite the weak performance delivered by software stocks, they are still far from deep value territory. Shares in the IGV ETF currently trade at a Price/Earnings multiple of roughly 42x. This is higher than the close to 30x Price/Earnings multiple average for IGPT. If hardware and chip stocks see faster profit growth, this could result in a continued performance divergence in favor of IGPT. One advantage IGV has is a slightly lower expense ratio of 0.39%, compared to IGPT's 0.56%.

Risks

We do believe there is real AI disruption risk for many software stocks, but not for all. We would therefore avoid a software focused ETF like IGV, and rather look at individual companies and their real disruption risk. Smaller, less entrenched software companies are likely to be the ones most at risk, compared to giants like Microsoft. We see a valuation risk with both ETFs, as the average valuation appears difficult to justify in both cases.

Both ETFs have stocks that are trading based on hypergrowth narratives, which makes them highly susceptible to a sharp price readjustment. A good example is Oracle (ORCL), where sentiment toward the stock quickly changed from an AI beneficiary, to a high-risk stock overly exposed to AI data center infrastructure.

Takeaway

The key insight we got from comparing these two ETFs is that the market has started to take software AI disruption more seriously. The performance divergence that really began in the second half of 2025 has accelerated. Comments like those from NVIDIA's Huang at Davos add credibility that AI coding is getting more powerful. Still, we believe it is a mistake to treat all software companies equally, as their competitive moats are different, and some could even benefit from AI if they incorporate its use in a smart way. With chipmakers, we see a risk that valuations could be pricing too much growth. In the case of software, we believe the AI disruption risk is real for many companies.

data.title: FuboTV: Huge Scalability News, Need To Profits First
data.description:
FuboTV Inc. (FUBO) is a U.S.‑based live streaming television service that started in 2015 with a focus on live sports and has since expanded into a broader sports, news and entertainment platform. The company aims to replace traditional cable TV by aggregating premium channels, including major sports packages, via internet streaming across the United States, Canada and Spain. The company’s core business has centered on attracting paid subscribers to its live TV service.

They have historically struggled with profitability and slender subscriber growth in a fiercely competitive streaming landscape dominated by larger players like YouTube TV. The stock is down massively since its IPO a decade ago and has dropped another 45% since this past September.

FUBO dropped some big news that is coming to life here in early 2026 on an agreement that could be the engine the company needs to start generating profits. However, I am still leery about the past performance on this stock based on slow revenue, weak cash flow, and the competitive market in attempts to gain subscribers. I have FUBO as a hold but am very curious to see how synergies play out with the new finalized agreement.

Hulu + Live TV

Earlier this month, FUBO came to an agreement with Disney to combine with their Hulu + Live TV segment to form a combined virtual Multichannel Video Programming Distributor (MVPD). The discussion began earlier in 2025, and received shareholder approval this past fall, with finalizations now taking place in Q1 2026.

Under the agreement, Disney will own 70% of FUBO. Operationally, FUBO will continue to run as its own streaming service, but now the stock represents ownership in Fubo and Hulu + Live TV business as a 30% stake. Management notes that the addition allows for greater consumer choice and flexibility, along with enabling the firm to strengthen their balance sheet and take strides toward positive cash flow.

FUBO shareholders will benefit from the synergies of the combination, and a jump in revenue should be expected. It will be interesting to see how the numbers roll out for Q1 2026 and the remainder of the year, and what the management tone is on synergies, as there really aren’t any initial number projections yet as to how this will affect sales, profits, and bottom-line. Nonetheless, this is an exciting time for FUBO, which was desperately needed as the stock has been riding the “dog days” for quite some time. It will be interesting to watch how this plays out for the stock, but even with the agreement, it’s hard to ignore the lack of growth organically.

Sales & Subscribers

The subscriber base will gain substantially on the books moving forward with the merger of Hulu + Live TV. Internally, though, subscribers were up 1.1% YoY, with loss of subscribers outside of their North America region. A service built on scaling subscriptions must show consistent and accelerating subscriber additions to justify its valuation and lack or profit, but FUBO has not be able to grow. Demand is seasonal and volatile with live sports, which remains a key element to their business. They have larger rivals that are hard to compete with, but the Hulu + Live TV should certainly help here, as now their combined subscriber base is near 6 million in North America.

Sales were growing quickly for FUBO in its earlier years, but have tapered off completely since the end of 2023, on stagnant subscriber numbers. The stock gained through 2020 and 2021 as revenue was climbing quickly but has since stalled. Prior earnings reports have mentioned pricing power as a problem in finding the happy medium of remaining competitive to consumers while trying to generate profits, as margins are stuck in the red. Hulu + Live TV will help with leverage and scale, as now, together combined with the current FUBO base, they are the sixth largest Pay TV service in the United States.

At the end of the day, revenue will clearly mount gains quickly once these synergies begin. I will be curious to see how investors react when these numbers arise, as well as excited to see how FUBO bottom-line (hopefully) improves as the agreement moves along.

Cash Flow

Operating cash flow has been consistently negative for years, and it has been a clear struggle for the business. Low subscriber growth, content costs, and lack of scale have been drivers to the inefficiencies of their core business. This is a big reason I don’t have the stock as a buy. I do really like what can happen with the Hulu + Live TV situation, but the internal battle to make profit year after year is very evident.

Moving forward, cash flow should be propelled by the new deal in several ways. Live TV is heavier in fixed costs like content contracts and tech infrastructure. With the addition of millions of total subscribers, these costs get spread over a much larger base, meaning more cash per subscriber. Hulu + Live TV will also bring a more premium entertainment audience and higher ad fill rates, which are generally higher in margin. Lastly, improving the audience base outside of sports, will help create more consistent cash flow too.

It's hard to predict what the numbers will look like, and most recently, FUBO was $-166.4 million for the year in cash from operations. I think there’s a good chance by end of fiscal year 2026, FUBO could be in the green for operational cash, but before buying this stock, I still need to see how a few quarters of earnings and trends look moving forward.

Conclusion

The combination with Hulu + Live TV introduces a credible path to greater scale, improved bargaining power with content partners, and potential cost efficiencies that Fubo could not realistically achieve on its own. If executed well, the deal could meaningfully strengthen the company’s competitive position and improve long-term economics in an otherwise unforgiving live-TV streaming market. That strategic optionality gives the stock upside that should not be ignored.

At the same time, meaningful risks remain. Fubo’s internal organic growth in revenue and subscribers has been modest, and the company has yet to produce consistent positive operating cash flow, suggesting structural challenges are a continuing weakness.

If cash flow and profitability soar through the first few quarters of 2026, I will be reconsidering a buy for this stock. Hold right now and wait to see how a few quarters play out from the big agreement.

data.title: UVIX: Hold Despite Macro Uncertainty
data.description:
Overview

The VIX futures term structure has been in contango for almost two months now, punishing UVIX, which is constantly rebalancing by selling close-to-expiry futures and buying more expensive longer-dated futures. Furthermore, the intraday VIX spike on January 15, driven by Fed and semiconductor export uncertainty, left UVIX down on the day, demonstrating the fragility of long-VIX products in generally low-volatility environments. In this article, I will explore the emergence of 0DTE options in the S&P 500 market as well as structural issues with UVIX, arguing that the product is not suitable in this environment.

The VIX tracks the 30-day implied volatility of the S&P 500, derived from the premiums of options with 23-37 days to expiry. Thus, any shorter-dated options are completely excluded from the index and thus don’t impact the VIX futures market. VIX spikes when traders buy protection either as a hedge or as speculation of higher volatility. This activity used to be concentrated on >30 days to expiry options; however, there has been a massive shift towards hedging short-term events using 0DTE. CBOE reports: “SPX zero day to expiry (0DTE) options trading has grown more than five-fold over the past 3 years, now averaging almost 2M contracts a day. What is driving that growth?... We estimate that retail now makes up around 50-60% of SPX 0DTE trading.” This was in part driven by the introduction of Tuesday/Thursday 0DTE expiry dates. Now, 0DTE makes up almost 57% of SPX options.

Thus, a massive portion of the market’s expectations is structurally detached from the VIX. Only longer-term increases in hedging activity or speculation can drive sustained VIX spikes. Since the liberation-day volatility of April 2025, the market has been relatively calm, particularly at the turn of the new year when VIX hovered between 13 and 15. Moreso, contango has persisted, typical of a calm market environment. As I mentioned in previous articles, VIX-linked ETFs are structurally disadvantaged amidst contango, as they are forced to sell cheap contracts that have suffered from time decay and buy more expensive contracts. In other words, they are holding futures while they decay. A large market of hedge funds and retail traders shorting volatility takes advantage of this dynamic, profiting in relatively calm market regimes with upward-sloping futures structures.

UVIX Implications

Any VIX-linked ETF struggles with this dynamic, but UVIX, a 2x leveraged ETF, naturally struggles even more than its less leveraged counterparts. Thus, there is a high bar to pass when arguing for a buy on UVIX. Specifically, there must be a probable catalyst, a favorable term structure, and reason to believe that the catalyst will impact longer-dated expiries as opposed to 0DTE markets. Anything short of this risks falling prey to the massive negative roll yield (the effect of buying expensive and selling cheap futures) endemic to UVIX. A telling example of this danger occurred in mid January. Currently, the term structure shows a February futures to spot spread of approximately 3, implying a 4-6% monthly decay, which is doubled by UVIX leverage.

On January 15, on the back of news of President Trump subpoenaing Jerome Powell and threatening Fed independence once again, in addition to the announcement of a 25% tariff on high-performance AI chips, the VIX spiked intra-day to 22.5. However, within hours, it collapsed back down to 15.84, and UVIX actually ended the day down. The short-term volatility was absorbed, and VIX mean-reverted extremely quickly.

Sustained volatility regimes invert the futures curve, creating backwardation, which benefits long-VIX ETFs (selling more expensive futures, buying cheaper futures). However, the futures structure has not experienced sustained backwardation since last April, a sign of a sustained low-volatility environment that has contributed to the massive decay of UVIX. This is closely connected to the January 15 event, as it demonstrates just how unique market conditions must be to make UVIX a smart hedge or speculation instrument.

Potential Upward Catalysts

Nevertheless, there are some compelling catalysts in the near future that contribute to an overall uncertain market condition. On January 21, the Supreme Court will hear a case that determines whether President Trump has the authority to fire Fed officials, marking a determining moment for US monetary policy independence. Secondly, on the same Wednesday, VIX futures and options expire, marking a rebalancing day for UVIX and other VIX-linked ETFs.

This creates a “pinning effect”, as market makers with large positions at certain VIX strikes are forced to constantly balance their hedges due to structurally higher gamma near expiries. This hedging leads to futures prices gravitating to wherever most expiries are. After the expiry date, this tethering ceases, and futures prices move more freely based on market events. Thus, there could be elevated activity post-expiry. Between January 27-28, the FOMC is also meeting to decide interest rates in one of its eight meetings per year. While there is a 84% chance that policy will be unchanged, changing forward guidance could induce volatility.

It could also be argued that the aforementioned low volatility in the last few months is artificially low due to an inflated AI trade and accommodative monetary policy despite above-target inflation. Generally, there is a case to be made to go long-VIX as a hedge to broad-based market exposure, or as a strategic directional bet on underpriced volatility.

While this represents a lower possibility, increased talk around US involvement in Iran and Greenland has created additional geopolitical uncertainty. While these represent longer-term, more unlikely events that are not as meaningful regarding a short-term instrument such as UVIX, they must be kept on the radar.

Structural Risks

As a general reminder, it is worth noting the risks inherent to a leveraged product such as UVIX. Particularly, the product experiences beta slippage over time, especially during volatile markets. The product is designed to be held for short periods of time, as a hedge or as a speculative instrument, evidenced by the dramatic value decay just YTD. With that being said, I view VIXY, a 1x VIX futures-linked ETF, as a more effective hedge due to its relatively lower beta slippage and lower negative roll yield. However, it is again worth noting that any long-VIX ETF experiences a level of decay amidst an upward-sloping VIX futures curve. The risky reality is closely linked to my earlier note regarding the high bar that must be reached in order to rate UVIX a "buy". Only when individual risk tolerance is high, roll yield dynamics are favorable, and macro headwinds indicate probable, underpriced volatility, is this bar met.

Due to its daily tracking and leverage, this is not meant to be a buy-and-hold product (more information about risks associated with this can be found here). Furthermore, even when it is appropriate to buy the product, the aforementioned structural risks mean that it should represent a small part of a portfolio.

Conclusion

For these reasons, I view UVIX as neither completely attractive nor unattractive at this time. The structural difficulties caused by higher 0DTE volume and persistent contango mean that the bar to enter this leveraged long-volatility product is extremely high. Yet, several meaningful near-term catalysts could create either short-term or long-term volatility. UVIX is highly sensitive to movement in longer-dated SPX options prices, and could easily spike on meaningful news linked to these events. Thus, I rate UVIX a hold.

data.title: Phoenix Education Partners: Attractive Valuation With A Clearer Business Model
data.description:
Investment action

I give a buy rating for Phoenix Education Partners (PXED) as I think the business model has gotten cleaner, margins should be structurally higher vs. history since it is now mostly virtual, and the existential regulatory risk has effectively been removed. Yet PXED trades at a discount vs. peers with similar growth but lower margin, which makes the risk/reward situation even more attractive.

Company Description

PXED is the parent company of the University of Phoenix, which has restructured itself into a digital-first education platform (nearly 100% virtual operating model). PXED currently serves ~85,600 students, focusing specifically on adults who are employed and need flexibility to upskill in fields like business, healthcare, and technology. This demographic is very different from the typical undergraduate (<18 years old), as they have significantly different economic backgrounds and demand profiles.

Q1 2026 Earnings Review

PXED reported a solid Q1 2026 quarter, with reported net revenue up ~3% y/y to $262 million. That may look weak to some investors, but I think context matters. Firstly, PXED is competing against a period distorted by fraudulent enrollments last year, and PXED has deliberately tightened intake standards this year. The fact that PXED was able to grow despite cleaning the book tells us that demand is real, and like-for-like growth would’ve been much stronger than this low-single-digit percentage. Aside, I also think the enrollment quality has gotten better. The bears would focus on the 1.2% y/y decline in average revenue per student as something bad, but I think otherwise. What happened was that PXED has shifted toward B2B partnerships, where pricing is lower but acquisition costs are close to zero. In other words, PXED is effectively trading headline ARPS for higher contribution margins, and I think that is a smart move. In terms of profitability, PXED printed adj. EBITDA of $75.2 million, with margins expanding 120 bps y/y to 28.7%, and adj. EPS of $0.4. This gave the board the confidence to declare a quarterly dividend of $0.21 per share.

Strategic Drivers and Valuation Catalysts

One of the key reasons to stay bullish on PXED is because the regulatory backdrop has significantly de-risked. For context, for-profit education names are regulated under the Department of Education’s Gainful Employment (GE) rules, and that was a major risk to the PXED business model because GE was literally an existential threat. If PXED fails the earnings tests, they lose access to federal funding, and the entire business model basically collapses. The good news is that, based on preliminary Department of Education metrics and PXED's own numbers, every reported PXED program is clearing the new earnings thresholds.

There are two implications here. One is that the risk of PXED losing federal funding is now off the table for the foreseeable future. Two is that it shows us the PXED education program works, and that is really what matters to me. Ultimately, PXED is an education service provider, and the metric that carries the most significance is student outcomes. PXED six-year graduation (note this is a standard federal metric) rate is now at 37%, which is up materially from historical levels (was 25% for the cohort entering 2015/2016). The better the track record of graduation, the more “credibility” PXED has, and that drives in more students to sign up, and because PXED is able to convert more students to graduates, it is effectively improving its customer lifetime value, since students are more willing to pay tuition for years knowing they are on the right track to graduation.

A school may be great at teaching, but it doesn’t mean the students will want to attend, but having such a satisfaction rate (the student satisfaction metric went up to 82%) tells us that PXED students like attending lessons. Higher satisfaction reduces churn and lowers remediation costs, and the most important element is that it drives strong word-of-mouth “marketing,” which is basically organic demand for PXED.

One of the most strategic decisions that PXED has made, which I agree with, is fraud mitigation. The problem today with online education is fraudulent or low-intent applications, which are really designed to get financial aid from the government. This doesn’t just make PXED look bad (if found out), but also makes it operationally complicated to facilitate schedules, classes, etc., since they do not have an accurate number of students. By implementing advanced identity verification, management did introduce friction to enrollment, but I think what PXED has gained is far more valuable, and that is credibility. The regulators know that PXED has no frauds in their student base, and PXED has a more holistic view of recurring tuition fees and the required cost to function. I’d say this makes the current revenue run rate far more predictable.

Valuation

With visible mid-single-digit growth, major risk off the table, and margins at >20%, I find it shocking that the stock only trades at 3.4x forward EBITDA. Let me give readers some numbers for comparison. American Public Education (APEI) and Strategic Education (STRA) trade at ~7.3x and 6.3x, respectively, a sharp premium vs. PXED. But despite that premium, they only have the same mid-single-digit growth outlook, have lower margins than PXED (peers with low-to-high-teens margins), and are all similar in size (APEI with ~$650 million LTM revenue and STRA with $1.3 billion). On a like-for-like basis, why should PXED trade at a discount? I think it is probably because PXED is a new IPO and the market is still catching up with expectations. But as PXED grows in line with peers, I expect the market to re-rate the valuation upwards. Assuming this trades up to 7x, the share price could potentially near double to $59.

Risk

The shift to online may have lowered PXED's cost structure, allowing it to scale faster, but it also lowers the barriers to entry since there is significantly less physical capex spending required. At worst, an influx of competitors could put pressure on pricing. PXED is also inherently counter-cyclical. A persistently tight labor market could weaken enrollment demand as students prioritize wages over upskilling.

Conclusion

PXED’s regulatory risk is largely off the table, enrollment quality has improved, revenue growth is much more visible, and yet the stock trades at just 3.5x forward EBITDA. The discount vs. peers doesn’t seem justified to me considering the relative growth and margin profile. I expect this valuation gap to close over time, and that could make the upside really attractive.

data.title: Renewables Growth To Be Driven By Shifting Priorities
data.description:
By Coco Zhang, ESG Research | Gerben Hieminga, Senior Sector Economist, Energy

We’ve entered an era in which electrons are fuelling technological advancement and economic growth. Solar and wind power, with their increasingly competitive costs, abundant supply, and clean nature, are well-positioned to play a vital role. In this outlook, we highlight our top three calls on the opportunities and risks for the global solar and wind market.

Call 1: Shifting priorities are driving robust solar and wind power generation

Electricity generation from solar and wind will grow steadily this decade. The International Energy Agency (IEA) projects that global solar and wind power generation will grow by 20% in 2026 and an average annual increase of 15% from 2026 to 2030 under its Stated Policies Scenario (STEPS), which reflects current policies and achievable policy goals. Bloomberg New Energy Finance is relatively more bullish, expecting 17% annual growth in both power generation and installed capacity in its Economic Transition Scenario.

Given policy and geopolitical uncertainties in some regions, we lean towards the IEA’s STEPS forecast. But both outlooks show robust growth despite the turmoil.

Solar and wind’s steady growth

Global solar and wind power generation forecast in TWh

China leads the growth in solar power generation, at 94% from 2026 to 2030. For wind, China and Europe both have the strongest growth outlook within the same period, at 50%. While the US outlook for solar is slightly higher than Europe’s, its weak spot is wind at only 19%.

China leads solar and wind development

Solar and wind power generation forecast by region in TWh

The main drivers of solar and wind growth have shifted beyond sustainability. AI, energy security, and strategic competitiveness have become the strongest engines.

  • For the US, the demand is driven by AI’s growing electricity needs. With shorter build times and fewer supply chain hurdles, renewables are becoming a ‘bridge’ until more dispatchable sources, such as gas and nuclear power, come online. Technology companies are also buying more renewable power to manage emissions from higher power use.
  • In Europe, growth is driven by energy independence. The Russia-Ukraine war pushed Europe to reduce its reliance on Russian gas and cope with higher gas prices, which in turn favours the buildout of renewables. Decarbonisation remains an important driver, though the focus has shifted to strategic autonomy, affordability and innovation.
  • China’s renewables market will be sustained by its absolute advantage in manufacturing solar and wind equipment – and its target to reduce emissions by 7-10% from peak levels by 2035. This means China will push full-speed ahead with renewables.

However, on the supply side, regions face different challenges in 2026.

  • Europe’s main risk is grid constraints from limited transmission and interconnection capacity, which can restrict deployment. On the positive side, member states will implement the EU’s new Grid Package to modernise infrastructure. Project economics remains solid generally, though offshore wind has weakened due to higher costs and lower power prices. This should drive wider use of ‘contracts for differences’ to stabilise revenues and improve auctions.
  • The US’s top risk is tariffs, which will reshape supply-chain partnerships (see Call 3) and permitting. This could mean tougher approvals and high-profile project cancellations, especially in offshore wind. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) is of less concern, as many top-tier developers have secured tax credits through 2030. Overall, we still expect solid market development despite lowered expectations.
  • China faces fewer supply chain and grid issues. Its challenge is adapting to last year’s shift from fixed tariffs to market-based auctions. While this will benefit the market in the long term, developers now face pressure to lower bidding prices and margins. Developers need to enhance cost efficiency, innovation, and strategic optimisation – key factors to watch this year.

Call 2: The pressing need for more renewables will boost the storage market

Addressing the intermittency of solar and wind power isn’t new, but 2026 will see a stronger push. More than ever, the industry sees adding battery energy storage systems (BESS) as a crucial solution for sustaining the future of solar and wind. Doing so can boost revenues by enabling a wider range of services and reducing curtailment. And in some cases, BESS is simply a prerequisite for building renewables in areas with grid congestion.

Thanks to advances in technology, BESS costs declined, disruptively, by 61% from 2020 to 2025.

Battery storage system costs continue to decline

Surveyed prices for turnkey BESS in $/kWh (real 2025)

We expect this trend to continue in 2026 and beyond. Combined with rising demand, it’s expected to result in a 31% annual increase in installed capacity through 2030. China is set to account for 46% of the global capacity in 2026, thanks to its advantages in BESS costs and deployment. This share will decrease slightly this decade as other countries ramp up production. In the US, for instance, a survey by Roth Capital Partners shows that major solar developers expect the share of storage attached to their projects to increase from 26% in 2026 to over 50% in 2030.

BESS deployment remains strong this decade

Global cumulative BESS installed capacity forecast in GW

These forecasts hinge on the assumption that geopolitical tensions do not escalate into a full-blown trade war over rare-earth elements critical to battery systems.

However, there are other challenges. The biggest, which we discussed in last year’s Energy Outlook, is the duration of battery discharge time. The batteries available today offer only four hours of discharge time – which isn't long enough. Long-duration energy storage solutions (LDES) offer promising potential but remain costly and limited, making them unlikely to have a major market impact in 2026. On the positive side, policy is supportive in many jurisdictions to increase the commercialisation of LDES. In the US, the OBBBA has preserved battery tax credits until the next decade, though the qualifying conditions would become tougher over time.

Lithium-ion phosphate (LFP) batteries remain the main choice for BESS, whereas electric vehicles will mainly rely on other lithium chemistries such as Nickel Manganese Cobalt (NMC) batteries. This is because LFPs have a longer cycle life and lower costs since they avoid nickel and cobalt. There's now increased development of sodium-based batteries, as sodium is more abundant and therefore cheaper than lithium. Sodium-based batteries will be a meaningful niche for BESS. Its market share will grow this decade but will likely remain in the mid-single digits.

Call 3: Strategic choices needed: boosting local production vs rapid and cheap expansion

Both Europe and the US have introduced policies to strengthen domestic solar and wind manufacturing. However, in the near term, shifting away from cheap Chinese products – which supply 98% of the EU’s solar panel imports – could raise costs, disrupt supplier partnerships, and slow installations. In 2026, balancing long-term industrial gains with short-term, cheaper imports will become more challenging.

To enhance domestic manufacturing, the US will rely heavily on tariffs, while the EU will focus on implementing the Net-Zero Industry Act (NZIA) as its primary tool, without tariffs.

For the US, tariffs will reshape solar and wind supply chains this year. In 2025, the US imposed steep solar tariffs – averaging 350% to 670% – on Cambodia, Thailand, and Vietnam, which supply a significant share of US imports. A 50% tariff was also imposed on steel-derivative products, which included wind towers and nacelles. Lastly, the US began investigating solar imports from India, Laos, and Indonesia, suggesting that additional tariffs could be imposed on these countries.

The EU’s risk of supply chain disruptions is lower because of limited tariff measures. But the bloc is wary of the lion’s share of Chinese solar equipment flowing in. In December 2025, Italy became the first EU country to have a solar auction that banned Chinese-made modules, cells, and inverters. We expect more countries to add auction rules in 2026, either through an outright ban on Chinese components or, more modestly, by prioritising EU-made components.

While these measures support domestic manufacturing, the benefits will take time to materialise. In the short term, solar manufacturing in both regions would be barely self-sufficient, though wind is in better shape. Plus, despite growing solar module capacity, cell and wafer capacity will remain low. This makes it difficult to establish a fully integrated supply chain.

Assessing the dependence of US and European solar and wind manufacturing on foreign production

Additionally, increased supply-chain turbulence awaits in 2026. For the US, while elevated tariffs can give domestic solar modules a price advantage, it can be time-consuming for developers to renegotiate supplier partnerships. It can be costly, too, as surging demand for US solar components can drive up prices.

For Europe, excluding Chinese or prioritising European components could raise project costs. In Italy’s case, by 17%, according to BNEF’s estimates. Meanwhile, funding under the NZIA appears insufficient to attract significant private investment. Therefore, EU solar manufacturing capacity is unlikely to grow substantially in the near future.

China, the global leader in renewables manufacturing, faces different challenges. Tariffs and overcapacity are hurting export competitiveness, squeezing margins and driving consolidation. These risks are expected to materialise in 2026 and persist for several years.

Taken together, despite uneven progress and varied challenges, solar and wind will continue to grow in 2026, strengthening their role in the global energy system.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Aflac: Priced For A Perfect Scenario
data.description:
My thesis

In my view, Aflac Inc. (AFL) is a boring, slow, and reliable insurance giant. Though in 2026, this stock is acting like a growth company. This is dangerous, even though operational results in Japan improved, because of cancer insurance products; the current share price includes all positive scenarios. Investors are paying a 30% premium for historically average growth.

Historically, AFL shares are traded at 10-11x P/E. This is a fair price for a slowly growing insurance company, which depends on interest rates and exchange rates. However, today's P/E is reaching 14.6x, which is 32% more expensive than the last 10 year average.

AFL is basically a Japanese company, paying taxes to the US, because around 70% of revenue comes from Japan. In 2025, Japan had a strong sales jump of 23.2% for new insurance premiums, the growth was mostly dependent on one time cancer insurance product "Miraito" cycle, and not a sustainable long term growth tendency.

In my view, AFL is financially a very strong company with a good balance sheet, but price is everything, and it is overpriced.

Business and product analysis

Aflac Incorporated's business model in 2026 is strictly split into two dominating segments, where Japan's markets, despite having slower growth, generate 55% of the whole company's yearly revenue of $18 billion. Their main product in Japan is cancer insurance, which makes up the biggest share in the portfolio and is deeply integrated in the needs of an aging population, where a quarter of households have an Aflac policy. On the other hand in US markets, where growth is quicker at 3-4%, the main revenue drivers are accident and short term disability insurances, distributed through employers worksite programs. An essential aspect of this product's use, distinguishing Aflac from traditional insurers, is the cash benefits model, which directly pays benefits to the insured and not to the medical institution. That's why customers use the money not for medical expenses, but rather to cover everyday expenses during illness. Financially, the company works like a giant investment fund with insurance benefits; its $108 billion investment portfolio generates a stable flow, though with Japanese Yen constantly weakening, it reduces real profits by converting them into dollars. Market accounts for this risk conservatively share is traded at a 14.3 P/E rate, which is just a little lower than the 5 year average, as well as a dividend yield of 2.2%, is still attractive for conservative investors searching for safe growth.

AFL's business outside Japan

Valuing AFL's business outside Japan, especially is US market, the company's growth profile is still moderate. The US segment is demonstrating slow growth, based on products distributed through employer channels and gradual product development, though here competition is a lot more intensive and the margins are lower than in Japan. Although the US gives some income diversification and stability, it does not change the picture for overall growth and does not reduce its dependence on currencies and macroeconomic factors. For this reason, operating results outside Japan improve business strength, though they do not create a structural growth pace, which would justify long term increases above historical levels.

Valuation and share price

Aflac's share price fluctuates between $108- $113, creating an odd valuation, which is hard to justify with fundamental measures. The current 14.2x P/E ratio is 35% over the 10 year historical average, which is around 10.5x. This suggests that investors pay $14.20 for every dollar of profit, hoping for growth, which this company doesn't show. If the AFL value went back to the historic median, the share price would have to drop to the 80-85$ level. Markets at today's price are paying for the perfect scenario, while ignoring the slow revenue growth.

What stands out even more is the price-to-book ratio being around 2.0x, while historically AFL is usually traded at 1.0-1.3x book value. These high multipliers, suggest that the market has already included a significant premium over the company's balance sheet value, leaving less space for unfavorable news or cycle changes. Going even deeper, P/B ratio is usually justified if the company manages to consistently generate high ROE and has sustainable growth potential. AFL ROE is not lower than the sector's average, though it is not high enough to justify 2.0x P/B without any long term catalyst. So valuing its book to book value, with a book value of $56 per share and using a proven 1.0-1.3x ratio AFL's fair price would be around $56-73, so today's prices of around $110 show no upside. Based on consensus expectations, AFL currently trades at roughly 13-14x forward earnings, still materially above its long-term historical average.

Value comparison

Aflac is currently trading at a high premium compared to its competitors. Aflac's P/E being at 14.6x doesn't make sense when looking at Principal Financial Group, which is traded at 11x P/E, and the difference is seen even more when comparing it with The Hartford Insurance Group, which stands at 10.3x P/E. This valuation gap is signaling that investors pay more just to have AFL's defensive supplemental health business model, even though PFG is offering a 3.8% dividend yield, compared to AFL's 2.1%. Valuing them from price to book ratio, AFL is at a 2.0x rate, also passing PFG with a 1.6x, and is equivalent to HIG at 1.95x. This suggests that the market is ignoring PFG's growth potential in the management segment due to its higher volatility. Essentially, you're paying a high premium for the shares when peers in the sector offer a better price-to-earnings ratio, it does not make financial sense at this stage of the market.

Capital return and downside protection

Aflac's capital return policy is one of the strongest in the whole insurance sector, though, because of that today it works more like a defence mechanism, rather than a price growth catalyst. The company consistently generates excess cash flows, of which a big portion is paid back to investors through dividends and aggressive share buybacks. In 2025, buybacks reached a few billion dollars, which implies that there are fewer and fewer shares in circulation every year. This policy stabilizes earnings per share and lowers the risk of cyclical profit fluctuation when unfavorable economic periods happen.

Dividend yield reaching 2%, with consistent buybacks, makes Aflac attractive as a defensive position in a conservative portfolio. Though these returns have more of a downside protection logic rather than a growth story. The current share price has fully accounted for the benefits from capital returns, which is why without any structural growth catalysts, it is limiting further price increases rather than encouraging them.

Risks

The main risk of Aflac is a huge dependency on Japan's markets, which generates around 55% of the whole company's revenue. Therefore, any Japanese yen's weakening against the US dollar directly cuts revenue when it's converted to dollars in financial statements. In 2025 Q3, average yen rate was fluctuating around 146-148 per dollar line, and this forex battle always takes away the final results. Even though the company uses hedging instruments, their costs directly reduce net investment income. Also, many investors ignore the fact that Aflac is basically a Japanese bond fund with insurance activities, a big chunk around $100 billion, investment portfolio is Japanese government bonds. So, if Japan's central bank decided to start raising interest rates, these bond prices would drop, which poses a threat to capital ratios.

Another structural problem is demographic. Japan's society is aging the quickest in the whole world, and this naturally lowers the new client base. Sales growth in the market is slow and often only reaches 1-2% in a year, which doesn't compensate for inflation's impact on costs. Also, the P/E ratio being at 14.2x, which is higher than the historical average, means that this premium is not backed up by fundamentals when profit growth is expected to be single digit. Shares are expensive, while opportunities are limited due to a saturated market and currency risks, which the company cannot fully control.

What could make the stock attractive again?

Aflac would become a more attractive investment if the share price came down to its historically average value or if there was a clear, structural catalyst for growth that outpaced the current product cycle. In practice, this would imply that price would need a correction to levels more relative to 10-12x P/E and a 1.2-1.4x P/B or sustainable proof that Japan's segment can grow quicker than the demographic tendencies let us believe. But until then, current share price reflects an almost ideal scenario for this company, rather than an attractive entry point for new capital.

My thesis summary

In my opinion, Aflac's share price in January 2026 does not match fundamentals and is overvalued. The market at the current rate is paying a 30% premium for slower growth, while the P/E ratio is 14.6x, even though historically the average ratio is 10.5x. Current optimism is built on a one time sales jump of 23.2% in Japan, which was driven by the Miraito product cycle rather than a sustainable long-term trend. The P/B ratio being around 2.0x, suggests that the share price is twice as high as the book value, even though this ratio is usually around 1.0x-1.3x. This leaves very little room for mistakes, especially when 55% of sales are coming from the aging Japanese market and yen exchange rate risks. Although a 2.2% dividend yield and share buybacks work as a safety measure, they don't justify the current price of $110. The real price of this company, based on historical multipliers, is somewhere around $80-$85, therefore, the current price reflects a perfect scenario and not the real value.

data.title: MRNY: Jumping On Big Moderna Move
data.description:
YieldMax MRNA Option Income Strategy ETF (MRNY) is one of the earliest of the covered call ETFs, having started trading in October 2023.

Not only is it one of the oldest covered call ETFs, but it has been among the worst performers in relation to real NAV erosion, collapsing from its all-time high of approximately $223.00 per share (post-reverse split of 1:10) to its all-time low of $13.60 (post reverse split) on November 24, 2025.

Since the all-time low, MRNY has made a big move, soaring to $22.84 per share as I write on January 2, 2026.

During that time when MRNY moved from paying monthly distributions to weekly distributions, the total payout dropped significantly, making the risk of holding a position not worth taking.

In this article we'll look at the major reason for the resurgence of MRNY, and how it flies in the face of mostly growth investors that assert the upside of the share price is very limited because of the inherent cap limits related to covered calls.

We'll also look at its distributions and what the future looks like for sustainable payouts.

Distributions

MRNY switched to weekly distributions on October 16, 2025. From that time up to November 28, 2025, distributions remained flat.

During that time the adjusted amount was multiplied by ten in each case, up until adjustments stopped on the payout starting on December 4, 2025.

Since the beginning of 2026, distributions have increased weekly, starting with $.1601 on January 2, and the latest distribution jumping to $.3737. That said, they did fall to $.1601 to start the year, making the four-week boost in weekly distributions less impressive because of the lower baseline they started from.

This isn't surprising since Moderna (MRNA) started its steepest climb in early January, although it took MRNY about a month to catch up with the initial increase in MRNA's share price, which began on November 20, 2025.

The good news is the positive response to the underlying strength of MRNA not only brought about the nice move for MRNY, but also the increase in distributions, reinforcing the fact these are how covered call ETFs are structured to perform.

Why MRNA took off

The first positive catalyst for MRNA was the result of reports circulating concerning MRNA was in talks with a large biopharma company concerning a partnership or buyout. That's what started the upward move in November.

While declining revenue from vaccines has been the major negative catalyst for MRNA, it did surprise to the upside with Q3 2025 revenue surpassing expectations. Of that revenue, $971 million came from COVID vaccine sales.

It was reported on December 12, 2025 that the European Medicines Agency (EMA) recommended the redesigned COVID shot of MRNA.

More positive December news came when the Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations (CEPI) said it would invest up to $54.3 million to support MRNA's Phase 3 trial for its experimental bird flu shot. That was especially helpful because of the decision by the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) to terminate a $766 million contract that had been awarded to MRNA concerning vaccine studies concerning several possible pandemic viruses.

More recently, it was announced that the combination of MRNA's mRNA cancer therapy, intismeran autogene (mRNA-4157) and Merck's (MRK) anti-PD-1 therapy, Keytruda, slashed mortality by 49% in the mid-stage trial.

Finally, the company is transitioning from its high exposure to vaccines to a portfolio that focuses on other areas of disease.

As a result of these positive catalysts, MRNA projects a growth rate of 10% for 2026.

The combination of these brought about the nice move starting in November 2025 and continuing to this day.

Since single covered call ETFs like MRNY will perform in alignment with the performance of the underlying, it's important to have a solid grasp of that underlying in order to make an informed decision on whether or not to take a position.

Lessons from a former(?) ultra-yield dog

I want to bring up some important things in regard to covered calls in general, and MRNY in particular.

First, a lot of growth investors like to point out that a covered call ETF like MRNY has its upside capped because of the way they work. While it's true covered calls, in and of themselves, are capped to the upside, that doesn't mean the share price of an ETF like MRNY is capped as well. That's evident in the way it has been performing in November, and especially January.

The major thing to take into consideration, as I have mentioned a number of times in my articles covering covered calls and other ultra-high-yield ETFs, is the price movement of an ETF like MRNY, when based upon the performance of the underlying, is different than actual NAV erosion. They're two different things, with the significance being if MRNY is dropping because of the performance of MRNA, it has a good chance to capture more of the upside when it reverses direction, than if the collapse in share price was solely coming from NAV erosion.

Also important is the fact that the distributions are rebounding along with the surge in share price of MRNA, and by extension, MRNY.

Conclusion

What I consider to be the most important factor to consider if thinking of taking a position in MRNY should be in relation to the abovementioned positive catalyst associated with MRNA. There is some substance there, and if it is able to continue on with a sustainable growth trajectory, MRNY will benefit from it significantly.

On the other hand, it should be understood that a lot of the catalysts are already priced in, and both MRNA and MRNY are likely to experience a correction of some type. Even so, if the catalysts largely hold, I don't think we're going to see the kind of sustained decline the two have experienced for some time.

I would like to see a better price point at this time for MRNY. It appears MRNA has turned the corner, and if it is able to hold and grow at a moderate pace, MRNY is going to do very well for investors.

With the solid distribution paid by MRNY, even if it were to trade sideways or slightly up going forward, it will be a strong income holding for those seeking a consistent revenue stream on a weekly basis.
